combined.worldview:
# Worldview

## Identity


## Settled Ground (8 claims)

- [BOULDER] "Claim_BoltOnBroaderImpacts": Direct quote from Dr. Marcus Williams: 'When I see our method requires field validation in underserved watersheds, which creates natural partnerships with tribal colleges who monitor those same systems — that's a broader impact that's also better science.' Contrasted with 'we will mentor two undergraduates' as a failure signal.
  supports: Narrative_IntegratedBroaderImpacts
- [BOULDER] "Claim_BudgetReadsLikeProjectPlan": Direct quote from Dr. Rachel Okafor: 'The PIs who get funded consistently are the ones whose budgets read like a project plan. Every dollar has a reason, and the reasons connect to the narrative in the project description.' Conviction: Boulder — practitioner with institutional vantage point, consistent with reviewer-facing framing in other samples.
  supports: Narrative_BudgetAsProjectPlan
- [BOULDER] "Claim_CopyPasteDetection": Direct quote from Dr. Marcus Williams: 'If I read three proposals from the same university and they all mention the same summer program, I know none of those PIs actually thought about broader impacts. They copy-pasted from the last funded proposal in their department.'
  supports: Narrative_InstitutionalCopyPaste
- [BOULDER] "Claim_GutReactionFirstPage": Direct quote from NSF reviewer: 'I'll be honest — by the time I finish the specific aims, I've already decided if this is a fund or a decline. Everything after that either confirms my instinct or occasionally changes my mind. But changing my mind takes work, and I'm reading 35 of these.' Conviction: Boulder — first-person practitioner account corroborated by panel of 4 reviewers.
  supports: Narrative_SpecificAimsPrimacy
- [BOULDER] "Claim_OnesentenceAdvocacy": Direct quote from NSF reviewer: 'I can only fight for three proposals in the panel discussion. I fight for the ones I can explain in one sentence to the other panelists. If your proposal doesn't give me that sentence, I can't advocate for you.' Conviction: Boulder — panel advocacy constraint is structural and corroborated by multiple reviewers.
  supports: Narrative_HolisticScoringHeuristic
- [BOULDER] "Claim_PostdocEffortMismatch": Direct quote from Dr. Rachel Okafor: 'I've seen brilliant science get triaged because the budget asked for a postdoc at 100% effort but the timeline showed three parallel workstreams. The reviewer does the math. If your budget doesn't add up, your project plan doesn't either.' Illustrates how effort-timeline mismatches trigger triage.
  supports: Narrative_VagueBudgetVaguePlan
- [BOULDER] "Claim_ReviewerMentalModelGap": 'Your significance section isn't about your research. It's about the reviewer's understanding of the field. You're not arguing that your work matters — you're showing them a gap in their mental model they didn't know existed.' Direct quote from Dr. Sarah Chen.
- [BOULDER] "Claim_SignificanceSectionFailureMode": 'The significance section is where most proposals fail — not because the science is weak, but because the PI doesn't connect their work to the reviewer's frame of reference.' Attributed to Dr. Sarah Chen, NSF panel member.
  supports: Narrative_ReviewerFrameOfReference

## Open Stakes (6 claims)

- "Claim_AntiPatterns": Three anti-patterns identified: (1) statistics lists, (2) 'no one has studied X' claims, (3) background sections relabeled as significance sections.
- "Claim_ExistingRelationshipsEvidence": A letter of support from a community partner already worked with is worth more than five paragraphs of plans. Evidence of existing relationships is the strongest signal of credible broader impacts.
- "Claim_SpecificityBeatsAmbition": Practitioner advice: 'we will partner with Diné College's water monitoring program' beats 'we will engage underrepresented communities'. Specificity signals genuine planning and existing relationships.
- "Claim_SuppliesLumpingSignal": Lumping supplies into a single line item signals the PI has not planned the experiments. Reviewers interpret this as a planning failure, not a budget formatting choice. Conviction: Stake — practitioner advice, plausible but not independently corroborated.
- "Claim_ThreePartStructure": The gap-consequence-unlock structure is presented as practitioner advice, not empirically validated. Conviction level Stake: plausible and actionable but not yet corroborated by independent evidence.
- "Claim_TriageAntiPatterns": Four triage anti-patterns enumerated by NSF reviewers: aims-as-tasks, significance-as-background, self-citation as innovation gap, budget disconnected from experiments. Conviction: Stake — practitioner enumeration, plausible and consistent with prior samples but not independently corroborated across sources.

## Actors (3)

- Dr. Marcus Williams — Retired program officer, NSF Division of Biological Infrastructure. Source of practitioner insight on broader impacts review criteria.
- Dr. Rachel Okafor — Grants administrator, Johns Hopkins University. Source of practitioner insight on budget justification as a hidden scoring criterion in grant proposals. Delivered internal training session for junior faculty on budget red flags, 2025-01-20.
- Dr. Sarah Chen — Former NSF review panel member; speaker at Grant Writers' Collective annual conference, 2024-09-15. Source of practitioner insight on grant significance sections.

## Key Narratives (13)

- "Narrative_BudgetAsProjectPlan": Reviewers use the budget justification as a proxy for the PI's execution competence. PIs who get funded consistently produce budgets where every dollar has a reason and those reasons connect to the narrative in the project description. The budget is the only place reviewers can verify whether the PI actually knows how to execute.
- "Narrative_EffortMilestoneAlignment": Effort allocations should match milestones: if Year 2 is the field season, that is where travel and personnel costs should concentrate. Mismatches between effort allocation and timeline milestones are a red flag reviewers notice.
- "Narrative_EquipmentCapabilityGap": Equipment should be justified by a specific capability gap, not generic need. Example: 'the nearest accessible instrument is 200 miles away and our samples degrade within 4 hours' is a strong justification; 'we need a mass spec' is not.
- "Narrative_GapConsequenceUnlock": (1) Gap statement: what's missing, framed as a tension the reviewer already feels. (2) Consequence frame: what cannot be done without closing this gap. (3) Unlock: what becomes possible once the gap is closed, in terms the reviewer's own work connects to.
- "Narrative_HolisticScoringHeuristic": Reviewers do not score each section independently and average. They have an overall impression and then find evidence to support it. A strong aims page with mediocre methods still gets funded; a mediocre aims page with brilliant methods rarely recovers. One memorable phrase or framing can carry the entire review.
- "Narrative_InstitutionalCopyPaste": Universities push PIs toward shared outreach programs (REU, STEM pipelines), resulting in identical broader impacts sections across proposals from the same institution. Reviewers notice repeated program names and infer the PI did not genuinely engage with the requirement.
- "Narrative_IntegratedBroaderImpacts": Reviewers can immediately distinguish proposals where broader impacts are structurally embedded in the research (e.g., field validation requiring community partnerships) from those where impact is a post-hoc addition. The best proposals make the impact inseparable from the science.
- "Narrative_ReviewerFrameOfReference": Reviewers read 30–40 proposals per session and are looking for the one that makes them say 'oh, this matters because…'. The significance section must show a gap in the reviewer's existing mental model, not argue for the research's importance in the abstract.
- "Narrative_SignificanceSectionFailure": Common failure modes: statistics lists, 'no one has studied X' claims (read as 'maybe there's a reason'), and background sections relabeled as significance sections.
- "Narrative_SpecificAimsPrimacy": Every reviewer develops a gut reaction in the first 90 seconds. The rest of the review is either confirming or fighting that reaction. Changing a reviewer's mind after the aims page requires work — and reviewers are reading 35 proposals. The specific aims page is therefore the highest-leverage page in the proposal.
- "Narrative_SpecificityOverAmbition": Specificity (named institutions, existing relationships, letters of support) signals genuine commitment and feasibility. Vague language about 'underrepresented communities' is a red flag for reviewers.
- "Narrative_TriageTriggers": Four triage triggers identified by NSF reviewers: (1) Aims that are really tasks, not questions. (2) Significance that is really background. (3) Innovation claims that cite the PI's own prior work as the field gap. (4) Budgets that suggest the PI has never actually run the proposed experiments.
- "Narrative_VagueBudgetVaguePlan": A vague budget signals a vague plan. Reviewers infer from budget incoherence (e.g., postdoc at 100% effort with three parallel workstreams in the timeline) that the project plan itself is not credible. The budget justification is the only part of the proposal where reviewers can see whether the PI actually knows how to execute.

## Hub Entities
- Narrative_SpecificityOverAmbition (12 connections)
- Narrative_VagueBudgetVaguePlan (12 connections)
- Narrative_BudgetAsProjectPlan (10 connections)
- Narrative_HolisticScoringHeuristic (10 connections)
- Narrative_InstitutionalCopyPaste (10 connections)
- Narrative_IntegratedBroaderImpacts (10 connections)
- Narrative_ReviewerFrameOfReference (10 connections)
- Narrative_SignificanceSectionFailure (10 connections)

layer0.name: Core Narrative + Style
layer0.description: Mission, Vision, PositioningThesis, key actors, stakes, aggregated style

layer1.name: Essential Context
layer1.description: High-value nodes (referenced 3+ times, rich content 100+ chars, summary predicates, core-related)
layer1.useCase: Most content generation, standard AI tasks

layer2.name: Full Semantic Content
layer2.description: All domain content with full attribution. Excludes only internal pipeline bookkeeping

layer3.name: Full Context with Metadata (TriG)
layer3.description: Complete graph with named graphs per transaction. prov:wasGeneratedBy implicit in graph structure.

graph:core.name: Core + High-Value (TriG)
graph:core.description: Core concepts + high-value nodes in TriG with named graphs per transaction

graph.name: Full Graph (TriG)
graph.description: Complete graph with named graphs per transaction
graph.useCase: RDF extraction, before/after diff, full structural analysis
